paragraphs.original_text: Curiosity Textbook of Science for Grade 2 The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye Probe and ponder z Have you ever wondered what you might see if the invisible world around you became visible? z How do you think your observation of this hidden world might change the way you think about size, complexity, or even what counts as living ? z Have you thought how these tiny living beings interact with each other? z Share your questions ?

Share your questions?

paragraphs.original_text: Diseases can be grouped into two major types based on their causes and how they spread: z Non-communicable diseases Some diseases, like cancer, diabetes, or asthma, are not caused by pathogens and do not spread from one person to another.

paragraphs.summary_text: Diseases can be grouped into two major types based on their causes and how they spread. Non-communicable diseases are not caused by pathogens and do not spread from person to person.

paragraphs.original_text: As we have learnt earlier (in the chapter Exploring Magnets in Curiosity, Grade 6), the compass needle is a tiny magnet which deflects when a magnet is brought near it and this magnetic effect can act through any non-magnetic materials kept in between.

paragraphs.summary_text: As we have learnt earlier (in the chapter Exploring Magnets in Curiosity, Grade 6), the compass needle is a tiny magnet which deflects when a magnet is brought near. This magnetic effect can act through any non-magnetic materials kept in between.

paragraphs.original_text: Chapter Electricity: Magnetic and Heating Effects The magnetic effect of electric current has many practical applications, such as in devices like electromagnets, electric bells, motors, fans, loudspeakers, and more. 4.1.1 Electromagnets .2: Let us explore z Take around cm long length of a flexible insulated wire, an iron nail, an electric cell, and few iron paper clips. z Tightly wrap the wire around the nail in the form of a coil, as shown in , and secure it with an adhesive tape. z Connect the ends of the wire to the cell.
paragraphs.simplified_text: Electromagnets have many practical applications, such as in devices like electrobells, electric bells, motors, fans, loudspeakers, and more . z Wrap the wire around the nail in the form of a coil, as shown in, and secure it with an adhesive tape . connect the ends of the wire to the cell, and attach it to the nail with some iron paper clips .
paragraphs.summary_text: The magnetic effect of electric current has many practical applications. electromagnets, electric bells, motors, fans, loudspeakers, and more.

paragraphs.original_text: Our scientific heritage Curiosity Textbook of Science for Grade Let us explore how these attractions vary in different states. 7.2.1 Solid state How are constituent particles held together in solids? .3 : Let us find out z Collect a few solid objects, such as a piece of iron or an iron nail, a piece of rock salt, a stone, a piece of wood, a key, and a piece of aluminium (). z Observe their shapes and sizes. z Try hammering them. z In which of the above six objects do you think particles are strongly held together?

chapters.chapter_title: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures

paragraphs.original_text: The staircase you use, the air you breathe, the water in your bottle, the food in your lunch box, the clothes and shoes you wear, the book you read, the trees outside, the ball you play with and even the stick you carry all of these are examples of matter, which you have learnt in earlier grades.
paragraphs.simplified_text: The stairs you use, the air you breathe, the water in your bottle, the food in your lunch box, the clothes and shoes you wear, the book you read, the trees outside, the ball you play with and even the stick you carry all of these are examples of matter, which you have learnt in earlier grades. You do not need to be a teacher or a mathematician; you must be able to understand what you are learning.
paragraphs.summary_text: The staircase you use, the air you breathe, the water in your bottle, the food in your lunch box, the clothes and shoes you wear, the book you read, the trees outside, the ball you play with and even the stick you carry all of these are examples of matter

paragraphs.original_text: Let us confirm the presence of carbon dioxide in the air. .1: Let us experiment z Take a glass tumbler and fill it half with water. z Add a small amount of calcium oxide (quick lime) slowly to it. z What do you observe? z Calcium oxide reacts vigorously with water to form calcium hydroxide and releases heat.
paragraphs.simplified_text: A small amount of calcium oxide (quick lime) reacts vigorously with water to form calcium hydroxide and releases heat . z Calcium oxide reacts strongly with water, releases heat and creates calcium chloride . the reaction is similar to that observed in carbon dioxide's atmosphere . it is not known whether or not carbon dioxide is present in the air .
paragraphs.summary_text: Calcium oxide reacts vigorously with water to form calcium hydroxide and releases heat. Let us confirm the presence of carbon dioxide in the air.

paragraphs.original_text: Mirrors, whose reflecting surfaces are spherical are called spherical mirrors. paragraphs.original_text: The outline of the surface of the mirror is circular. (a) (b) : (a) Concave mirror; (b) Its representation (a) (b) : (a) Convex mirror; (b) Its representation A spherical mirror which has a reflecting surface that curves outwards is called a convex mirror (a).

The shape of the when light bounces off a surface surface of a mirror is described as round or oval . it is also known as a cylindrical mirror, or a rectangular mirror .

paragraphs.original_text: They all interact with the other living beings and non-living things present in the places where they grow and thrive. Habitats are all living organisms that interact with other living beings and non-living things present in the places where they grow and thrive .

paragraphs.original_text: In this way, we can observe and record populations of different kinds of organisms in a single habitat. .2: Let us record We can understand the population of a particular type of plant by counting them at a given place and time. z Divide students into four to five groups. z Each group may identify any two organisms, plant(s) or animal(s). z Mark an area of m m in your school garden. z Identify four organisms in this area, and count their numbers. z Record the number of the organisms in . z Compile the data from all groups.

paragraphs.summary_text: In this way we can observe and record populations of different kinds of organisms in a single habitat. We can understand the population of a particular type of plant by counting them at a given place and time.

paragraphs.simplified_text: 3.: Interesting features about the Earth Discuss the features you have listed with your teacher and friends . 4.: We can stand on the ground held by gravity (as we learnt in Chapter 5), but our heart can pump blood up to our head . 5.: Our Heart Can Pump Blood Up to Our Head . 6.: The Earth's magnetic field is one of the most important parts of life .
